Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or until something was due soon. I'm 25, so I've been through the university system long enough to know how to get by without killing myself over it. I'd maybe dedicate a few hours here and there throughout the week, but nothing too structured. Visual stuff like diagrams and videos worked best for me, so I'd prioritize those when I actually sat down to study.
Q2: It definitely varied depending on what was coming up. I didn't have a set schedule like 'study every Tuesday at 3pm' or anything like that. When deadlines approached, I'd ramp up, but otherwise I kept things pretty chill. My extracurriculars and other life stuff meant I had to be flexible anyway. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd put in more effort.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check if something was due or to watch a video for an upcoming assignment. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember that I hadn't logged in for a bit. I wasn't one of those people constantly refreshing the page. It was more functional than anything—get in, get what I need, get out.
Q4: Yeah, after a while it became kind of automatic to check in every few days. I think it happened because the course had regular content drops and I didn't want to fall too far behind. It wasn't exciting or anything, but it became part of my weekly rhythm. Like checking email or social media, just another thing to glance at.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them at my own pace and rewind if something didn't click. The discussion boards were also decent because I could see how other people were thinking about the material. Anything that let me absorb information without reading walls of text was a win in my book.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the extra readings and any optional supplementary materials. They felt like busywork to me, and I didn't have the motivation to go above and beyond when I knew I could pass without them. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably skipped it. My resources are limited, so I had to be strategic about where I spent my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be graded on. If I could see the payoff—like understanding a concept for the exam or completing an assignment—I'd engage with it. But if it was just 'enrichment' with no clear purpose, I'd zone out. I need to see the point, otherwise it's just noise.
Q8: Optional activities felt exactly that—optional. I treated them like suggestions rather than requirements. Required stuff got done because it had to, but optional things only got my attention if I was genuinely curious or had nothing better to do, which was rare. There's a big mental difference between 'you should' and 'you must.'
Q9: Yeah, absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my laptop and study from there without being online. I also used my own notes and sometimes YouTube videos that explained things better than the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me with my notes and some coffee.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing downloaded content or working through problems on paper, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd spend hours going over visual diagrams I'd printed out or sketched myself. The LMS only sees clicks and logins, not the actual thinking happening in my head or on my desk.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the platform, then I'd have it saved or I'd remember the key points and work from my notes. Going back to the LMS repeatedly felt inefficient when I already had what I needed. Once I extracted the information, I didn't need to keep returning to the source.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just felt bored with the whole thing. The content wasn't always stimulating, and sometimes life stuff—work, social commitments—made it hard to care. When the material felt repetitive or too easy, my engagement dropped. I'd do the minimum to keep my head above water.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor. Being 25 and juggling various responsibilities means I don't always have peak energy for coursework. When I was tired or stressed about other things, I'd just skim through materials quickly or postpone logging in. The LMS became something I'd deal with 'later' more often than I'd like to admit.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about other stuff. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing anything. Technically I was 'engaging' with the material, but mentally I was checked out. It happens when you're bored.
Q15: I'd postpone discussion posts and readings pretty regularly. They felt tedious, and I knew I could cram them in later if needed. Important or not, if something didn't grab my interest, it got pushed to the back burner. I'm good at last-minute work, so I relied on that skill maybe too much.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd see something was due in a few days and suddenly become very productive. Without deadlines, I'd drift. The LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined in between. That's just how I operate—pressure creates action.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but not a huge one. If I got good marks, I felt validated and maybe a bit more motivated. If feedback was critical, I'd note it but not dwell on it. I didn't really change my approach based on feedback; I just kept doing what worked well enough to pass.
Q18: The structure was okay—regular content releases kept me somewhat on track. But it also felt predictable and a bit monotonous after a while. Having clear deadlines helped, but the lack of variety in activities made it easy to disengage. I appreciated knowing what to expect, even if it got boring.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. But the heavy reliance on readings and text-based discussions made it harder to stay interested. If there had been more interactive or visual elements, I probably would have been more engaged. The design was functional but not exciting.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to figure out what's actually important without committing to the work. They're exploring the system, maybe procrastinating, or just logging in out of guilt without following through. High activity doesn't mean high productivity—sometimes it's just digital wandering.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I didn't need to be constantly logged in to do well enough. I'd grab what I needed, study offline, and show up for the important stuff. Efficiency over visibility. Some of us just know how to work the system without leaving a huge digital footprint.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work—the real studying that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine mental effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being present and curious. For me, engagement has been inconsistent this course. I engaged when it mattered for grades, but genuine intellectual engagement was rare.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm actually pretty resilient and capable when I need to be. It wouldn't capture my offline study habits or the fact that I can perform well without constant online presence. It also wouldn't show that my boredom isn't about inability—it's about the material not capturing my interest. I'm coasting, but I'm coasting competently.
